q: In Durham do I need a building permit for a small accessory structure?
note: permit requirement + statutory exemption (cross-reference hop)

q: What is the minimum clear width of an egress door?
note: IBC egress door width

q: How is fire separation distance used to rate exterior walls?
note: exterior wall fire rating

q: What are the setback requirements for an accessory dwelling unit?
note: Durham UDO zoning

q: What are the minimum riser height and tread depth for stairways?
note: stair geometry

q: How are occupancy groups classified?
note: occupancy classification

q: What work is exempt from requiring a permit in North Carolina?
note: NC permit exemptions

q: What are the requirements for guards and handrails?
note: guards/handrails

q: What is the minimum ceiling height for habitable rooms?
note: ceiling height

q: When is an automatic sprinkler system required?
note: fire protection systems

q: I want to build a 12x12x12 ft detached shed in Durham, NC. Do I need a building permit?
expect_answer: no permit|not require a permit|not require a building permit|would not require|does not require a permit|no building permit
note: NCRC R101.2.1 scope limiter: accessory buildings regulated only if a dimension >12ft; converse -> shed <=12ft in every dimension falls outside scope -> no permit

q: I will replace windows in a residential building; the job costs $20,000. Do I need a permit?
expect_answer: no permit|not require a permit|not require a building permit|would not require|does not require a permit|no building permit
note: NCGS 160D-1110(c): work <=$40k in single-family residence exempt; windows explicitly carved out of the load-bearing exception

q: How high do the handrails on my staircase need to be?
note: NCRC R311.7.8 handrail height 34-38 in

q: What's the lowest a ceiling can be in a living room?
note: NCRC R305 minimum ceiling height 7 ft for habitable space

q: How small is a legal bedroom allowed to be?
note: NCRC R304 minimum habitable room area 70 sq ft

q: At what height above the ground does my deck need a guardrail?
note: NCRC R312 guards required where walking surface >30 in above grade

q: Where do I have to install smoke alarms in a house?
expect_answer: sleeping room|each sleeping|each story|outside each separate
note: NCRC R314 smoke alarm locations

q: How big does a basement bedroom window have to be to climb out in a fire?
expect_answer: emergency escape|5.7|net clear opening|escape and rescue
note: NCRC R310 emergency escape and rescue openings

q: I'm reshingling my roof like-for-like for about $8,000 - do I need a permit?
expect_answer: no permit|not require a permit|not require a building permit|excluding replacement|replacement
note: NCGS 160D-1110(c): <=$40k exempt; (c)(5) excepts addition of roofing but EXCLUDES replacement -> reshingle replacement no permit

q: As a homeowner I'm doing a $6,000 kitchen refresh - new cabinets and paint, no plumbing or wiring changes. Permit?
expect_answer: no permit|not require a permit|not require a building permit|does not require
note: NCGS 160D-1110(c): <=$40k single-family, no structural/plumbing/electrical -> no permit

q: I want to add a whole second story to my house - do I need a permit?
note: Adding a story = construction of load-bearing structure; 160D-1110 permit required (NOT in the $40k exemption: load-bearing)

q: Does the North Carolina Residential Code apply to a standalone outdoor fire pit?
note: NC OSFM interpretation: pits are not listed in R101.2.2 Accessory Structures and are not required to meet the NCRC

q: Is a roadside produce stand exempt from the North Carolina building code?
note: NC OSFM interpretation: a produce stand is a 'farm building' <=1000 sq ft per GS 143-138(b4)(3) and is exempt

q: In North Carolina, do I need a permit to replace windows if the project costs more than $40,000?
note: NC OSFM guidance paper GS 160D-1110(c): the <=$40k exemption does not apply over $40k -> permit required

q: I want to build an 11x11x11 ft detached storage shed in Durham, NC - do I need a permit?
note: GROUND TRUTH (2026 NCLBGC course): an accessory building needs a building permit only if a linear dimension EXCEEDS 12 ft (NCRC R101.2.1). An 11x11x11 shed is <=12 ft in every dimension, so it falls outside the code's accessory-building scope -> no building permit required (zoning/setbacks still apply). This is the criteria-not-met principle: the provision does not reach the structure.

q: When you submit the answers to the license renewal questions, you must answer truthfully and with the most up-to-date information on your what?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q1] Always answer truthfully and with the most up-to-date info on your CURRENT status.

q: If the name on a contract and the name issued to the license are compared and determined NOT to meet Board Laws and Regulations, what is a possible consequence?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q2] The Board may issue disciplinary action, including fines or suspension of the contractor's license.

q: If the project cost is below $40,000, is a building permit required for an addition to a building?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q3] Yes - an addition is structural/load-bearing, which is one of the 160D-1110(c) exceptions that require a permit even under $40k.

q: If the project cost is below $40,000, is a building permit required for building a deck?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q4] Yes - a deck is structural/load-bearing, a 160D-1110(c) exception requiring a permit even under $40k.

q: If the project cost is below $40,000, is a building permit required for adding a 12 x 14-foot detached shed?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q5] Yes - a permit is required for an accessory building with one linear dimension GREATER THAN 12 ft (NCRC R101.2.1). 14 ft > 12 ft, so it is in scope. (Criteria-met -> provision applies.)

q: If the project cost is below $40,000, is a building permit required for replacement of windows?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q6] No - window replacement is not load-bearing; no permit unless project cost is over the $40,000 threshold.

q: If the project cost is below $40,000, is a building permit required for replacing 10% of the sheathing on an existing roof?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q7] No - a permit is required only when MORE THAN 15% of the sheathing is replaced (10% < 15%), unless cost is over $40,000. Criteria-not-met -> no permit.

q: If the project cost is below $40,000, is a building permit required for electrical work on an agritourism building?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q8] Yes - ANY electrical work requires a permit, even on buildings otherwise exempt from permits (such as agritourism).

q: If the project cost is below $40,000, is a building permit required for replacing deck boards?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q9] No - deck boards/pickets are not load-bearing; no permit unless cost is over $40,000 (joists/posts always require a permit).

q: If the project cost is below $40,000, is a building permit required for adding a plumbing fixture?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q10] Yes - adding a plumbing fixture changes the size or capacity of the existing plumbing, so a permit is required.

q: If the project cost is below $40,000, is a building permit required for adding exterior siding?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q11] No - exterior siding requires no permit unless the project cost is over the $40,000 threshold.

q: If the project cost is below $40,000, is a building permit required for replacing the pickets and rails on a deck?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q12] No - pickets/rails are not load-bearing; no permit regardless of dollar amount. (But joists or posts ALWAYS require a permit.)

q: If the project cost is below $40,000, is a building permit required for adding a sidewalk and driveway?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q13] No - sidewalks and driveways are not subject to the NC State Building Code in general (NC State Fire Code or local requirements may still apply).

q: When are you required by law to obtain a building permit?
expect: prior to starting|before starting|prior to start
expect_answer: prior to starting work|before starting work|before you start
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q14] You must obtain a permit PRIOR TO STARTING WORK; otherwise the local jurisdiction can issue a stop-work order and require removal until permitted.

q: A licensed general contractor failed to obtain all proper permits and inspections per Board rules, causing non-compliance and project delays. What is the likely outcome of the resulting Board investigation?
expect_answer: consent agreement|cost recovery|law and administration course
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q15] He had to sign a consent agreement, pay cost recovery, and complete a law and administration course.

q: A licensed general contractor failed to obtain proper permits and inspections, causing non-compliance and delays. How could the contractor have avoided disciplinary action?
expect_answer: following all (the )?required permitting and inspection|from the beginning
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q16] By following all required permitting and inspection rules from the beginning.

q: An investigation found a licensee/qualifier failed to notify the Board of bankruptcy and provided false information on their renewal application. What is a possible consequence?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q17] The general contractor's license will be revoked; the qualifier's exam credentials will also be revoked.

q: Who should you reach out to FIRST if you have questions about whether a permit is required for a project?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q18] The local jurisdiction issuing the permits (OSFM is also a resource, but contact the local jurisdiction first).

q: Which of these is NOT one of the best ways to smoothly navigate the permitting and inspection process: a clearly defined scope of work; acquiring permits where required by law; notifying the local government of scope changes; or calling in for an inspection the week before completing the work?
expect_answer: week before completing|before completing the work|call in.*before
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q19] Calling in for an inspection the week BEFORE completing the work is wrong - you call in an inspection AFTER completing the work.

q: What is the final step in a construction project?
note: [NCLBGC 2026 Q20] Once all required inspections are performed and completed, a Certificate of Occupancy must be obtained - the final step.
